stories.title: The Bone-Flute's Price
stories.text:
Valren the bone-singer apprentice found the old flute half-buried in the Ironspine foothills, its surface carved with syllables that made her teeth ache. The Elder Tongue. She should have left it. Instead, she carried it to her master, Mesek, whose hands had grown too gnarled to carve anymore. "Where did you find this?" His voice cracked like frost on stone. "Below the singing caves. It called to me."

Mesek's breath caught. He took the flute with fingers that trembled. When he raised it to his lips, the sound was not music—it was memory given voice. Fragments. Consciousness. The flute sang of a deepkin captain drowned three centuries past, her final oath unwitnessed, her name eaten by the Tear. Valren felt the weight of it: the dead demanding remembrance. The flute was not a tool. It was a debt made flesh. "Master, what does it want?"

"To be played at the boundary-waters," Mesek whispered. "To renew the covenant the old way—with willing death, not daily choice. The Tear is failing because the surface-dwellers refuse sacrifice.

stories.title: The Glasswright's Second Forge
stories.text:
Sella had been a journeyman glass-forger in Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter for seven years before the Remaking shattered the Dominion's furnace-songs. Now the kilns stood cold, the consciousness-fragments used as fuel were scattered like treasure, and the old furnace-magic no longer sang. So she learned to forge without it. In a hidden workshop beneath the weaver's quarter, Sella and five other craftspeople rekindled a kiln using only memory—the old songs their grandmothers had taught them, before the Dominion standardized everything into phantom-ledgers and profit-margins. No fragments. No Dominion script. Just bone and breath and the rhythm of the forge. The glass they created was imperfect, cloudy, sometimes cracked. But it held meaning. Each piece carried the maker's name carved into its base. When the young auditor—Venna, fresh-faced and torn between duty and conscience—found their workshop, Sella braced for seizure. Instead, Venna set down her ledger and asked: "Will you teach me?"

They did. Within a month, the workshop had doubled in size. The Dominion's version of glass-forging had required commodifying consciousness itself. This new forge demanded only honesty.

stories.title: The Wyvern-Lord's Mercy
stories.text: Sorrath had hoarded for three centuries in the Ironspine caverns—gold, jewels, consciousness-fragments, the weight of kingdoms—until the Remaking made it all dust. Now she sat alone in her crystalline vault, surrounded by worthless metal, contemplating the nature of meaning. Sorrath should have incinerated them. Instead, she watched. The old wyvern recognized something in their desperation that mirrored her own. She descended in her human form—a woman of burnished skin and eyes like molten copper—and offered them a bargain: work for passage through the mountains, and she would feed them. They accepted before fear could override hunger. Over weeks, the refugees taught Sorrath something her three centuries of hoarding had never revealed: wealth that could not be stolen, burned, or made worthless by cosmic catastrophe. A child learned to read from the eldest refugee. An injured woman healed by tending the cavern's luminous pools. A musician crafted instruments from bone and taught the others songs that made the stone echo with joy. When they departed, Sorrath did not ask for payment.

stories.title: The Spice-Merchant's Third Route
stories.text: Torvin had spent twenty years mapping the Scorched Reach caravan routes, profiting from knowledge others lacked. But when the Remaking fractured the trade-roads into three competing timelines, his ledgers became worthless—or rather, they split into three contradictory versions, each claiming to be truth. He stood in the Wailing Scar—that impossible market existing in liminal space—and made a choice: instead of choosing one timeline's ledger, he would trade between all three. The opportunity was unprecedented and suicidal. Merchants from the three timelines couldn't see each other's goods; only Torvin, standing at the threshold between realities, could bridge the gap. He bartered spice from one timeline for copper from another, swapped silk for starlight-glass, created profit not through scarcity but through translation. Within months, Torvin's caravan had become legend. But the Sovereignty—the state's surviving bureaucratic remnant—began hunting him. An auditor named Mara shadowed his every move, carrying ledgers that contradicted themselves, trying to understand how he could trade between incompatible realities. When they finally met in a waystation north of Pelimor, Mara didn't arrest him.

stories.title: The Locksmith's Vigil
stories.text:
Gorven had spent fifty years opening locks. Every mechanism in Keshtir's merchant quarter bore the marks of his picks—silent, surgical work that moved merchandise without triggering wards. After the Remaking, the Dominion's locks were useless; the consciousness-fragments they guarded had scattered like sand. But Gareth the Locksmith—the legendary master, imprisoned in Keshtir's deepest vault for refusing to forge new locks for the auditors—remained sealed behind a mechanism that only one person in the world could open. The metal was meteoric iron; the combination was carved in Elder Tongue syllables that shifted if touched wrong. It took him three hours. When the vault door swung open, Gareth was gone—but not escaped. He sat in the center of the chamber, waiting, his hands bound in silver chains that glowed with binding-magic. "You're the counter-offer," Gareth said, his voice hollow. "They knew someone would come. They bound me to the vault. If you free me, the collapse will kill us both."

"The binding is old," Gorven observed, examining the chains. "Three years at least.

stories.title: The Copper-Fingered Thief
stories.text: Marin had three days to reach Verdant Strand harbor before the auditors' net closed. Her copper fingers—forged through blood-debt and craft—could open anything: locks, vaults, sealed ledgers. But the auditors were hunting her now, and not for theft. They wanted her to open the harbourmaster's vault beneath Vel-Karsh. Inside lay the Tear of Ashglass, a fragment of the drowned god's mercy. The Dominion had seized it decades ago. Now, with the Tear of the Drowned God failing and the boundary-waters collapsing, the deepkin were demanding its return—willing to go to war if surface-dwellers refused. Marin reached the vault at midnight. The lock was Dominion-made, all meteoric iron and bone-resonance. She felt it sing as her copper fingers worked the mechanism. The auditors were three streets away. She could hear their boots. The lock opened. Inside: not the Tear, but a ledger. Written in blood-ink. It documented every fragment the Dominion had ever stolen from the deepkin's sacred waters. Seventeen items. Each one worth more than a province.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Mercy
stories.text: Velka had hoarded for three hundred years. Gold, pearls, consciousness-fragments, the stolen shadows of tyrants—all heaped in her mountain lair, each piece catalogued in a ledger older than kingdoms. The gold turned to dust. The fragments began singing. She found the child on the third day after the cataclysm, half-starved in the foothills, fleeing the Burned Expanse. A human girl, no more than ten winters, coughing ash from her lungs. Velka should have ignored her. Wyverns did not keep pets. Wyverns kept treasure. Instead, she brought the girl tea brewed from starflowers and fed her dried fish from her pre-Remaking stores. The girl asked no questions, only ate and slept in the warmth of the lair. "Why?" Velka asked herself, watching the child dream. The hoard answered: it did not. The gold was silent. The fragments had stopped singing weeks ago. When the child woke, Velka offered her a choice: stay, or be guided safely to Verdant Strand, where refugee caravans were gathering. The girl chose to stay, and in staying, transformed Velka's lair.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Oath
stories.text:
Valren was an apprentice bone-carver when the Remaking shattered the world, and she was fourteen when Naela—the eldest of the bone-singers—found her hiding in the ruins of Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter. The furnace-houses had burned. The glass-forgers had fled. And the Dominion auditors were hunting anyone who understood the old frequencies. "I cannot teach you everything," Naela said, her hands scarred from decades of carving pre-human syllables into bone. "But I can teach you what the dead refuse to forget."

For six months, Valren carved. She learned the Elder Tongue not from books or masters, but from listening—truly listening—to bone itself singing. Each syllable cut into the marrow released fragments of memory: pre-human songs, conversations between mountains and stars, the first covenant sworn between deepkin and surface-dwellers. Then Naela gave her the final task: carve the oath that would bind the refugee networks together. Not a blood-oath—those were Dominion magic, leverage-magic. A living oath, one that renewed each dawn through choice, not compulsion. Valren took her grandmother's bone-knife and began.

stories.title: The Reef-Keeper's Price
stories.text: Captain Vessa had been harbourmaster of Verdant Strand for seventeen winters, and in that time she had learned the secret the Dominion tried to hide: the Tear of the Drowned God was not eternal. It was failing. The boundary-waters trembled. Strange fish appeared in the shallows—not the merfolk kind, but something older, something that should have stayed in the abyss. The deepkin Captain Merenna, who had visited the harbormaster's tower for parley, had split into seven copies of herself, each giving different counsel, unable to agree whether to renew the covenant through blood-sacrifice or risk something unprecedented: a living daily choice. Vessa knew what the deepkin did not: she held the key. Thirty years ago, before the Remaking, she had dived the pearl-beds beneath the Sapphire Deep and stolen a fragment of the Drowned God's mercy—a small pearl that sang with the voice of the boundary itself. She had kept it hidden, worn beneath her heart. Now, with the Tear failing and war looming between surface and abyss, she dove again.

stories.title: The Shadowless Bargain
stories.text:
Gorven the Conjurer had lost his shadow years ago—sold it to a shade-merchant for pocket money and a fever dream. He'd forgotten the price. Now, with the Remaking shattering certainties, the merchant was calling in the debt. He sat across from her in the Lampwick Quarter's oldest tea-house, a place where three versions of Keshtir overlapped. The shade-merchant was beautiful in the way a knife wound is—precise and somehow welcoming. "Your shadow," she said, "is worth more now. Shadows carry memory in the new world. Yours remembers forty years of lock-picking, every vault opened, every chain broken. That kind of memory is currency."

Gorven sipped his tea. Without his shadow, he cast nothing at noon, and in darkness he was invisible. It had made him the perfect thief for the Counter-Archive's work, smuggling consciousness-fragments out of Dominion furnace-houses. "What if I don't pay?" he asked. "Then your debt compounds. By autumn, I'll own your hands. By winter, your voice. By spring—" she smiled "—your name."

Gorven stood.
